datasets.title: EU Credit Card Fraud Micro Transactions 2018–2022
datasets.description: Anonymized micro-level transaction records from a consortium of European banks, including flagged and confirmed credit card fraud cases between 2018 and 2022.

datasets.title: US Credit Card Fraud Transactions 2017–2021
datasets.description: Micro-level transaction dataset from a large US card issuer with labeled fraud and non-fraud transactions at the authorization level.

datasets.title: Bank A Internal Credit Card Fraud Logs
datasets.description: Internal fraud monitoring logs from a single European retail bank, including rule-based alerts and investigation outcomes.

datasets.title: Synthetic Credit Card Fraud Benchmark Data 2021
datasets.description: Fully synthetic credit card transaction data with injected fraud patterns designed for benchmarking fraud detection algorithms.

datasets.title: Real-Time Card Authorization Stream (De-identified)
datasets.description: Streaming-style extract of de-identified card authorization messages from an international processor, sampled at 1% of traffic.

datasets.title: Latin American Corporate Tax Evasion Panel
datasets.description: Firm-level unbalanced panel of tax declarations, third-party reports, and audit outcomes for selected Latin American countries.
datasets.geographic_coverage: Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, Peru

datasets.title: Household Survey on Informal Payments and Bribery
datasets.description: Cross-country household survey on informal payments, bribery, and perceptions of corruption in public services.

datasets.title: US Suspicious Activity Reports on Money Laundering
datasets.description: Aggregated and partially de-identified suspicious activity reports (SARs) related to money laundering filed by US financial institutions.

datasets.title: Global Bank Financial Statements 1995–2020
datasets.description: Bank-level balance sheet and income statement data harmonized across 140 countries, suitable for studying financial statement fraud indicators.

datasets.title: Global Ponzi Scheme Investor Network Dataset
datasets.description: Investor-level network data for a set of large Ponzi schemes, including referral links and investment timing.

datasets.title: Customs Declarations and Trade-Based Money Laundering Flags
datasets.description: Shipment-level customs declaration data linked with algorithmic flags for potential trade-based money laundering.

datasets.title: Digital Asset and Cryptocurrency Fraud Case Database
datasets.description: Case-level dataset of reported frauds involving digital assets and cryptocurrencies, including Ponzi schemes, ransomware, and exchange hacks.

datasets.title: Tax Gap Micro-Simulation Input Data
datasets.description: Anonymized individual tax return microdata prepared for tax gap estimation and behavioral response simulations.

datasets.title: Online Marketplace Seller Fraud and Reputation Data
datasets.description: Seller-level panel data from a large online marketplace, including fraud investigations, chargebacks, and customer ratings.

events.title: European Conference on Financial Crime & Compliance 2025
events.description: An interdisciplinary conference focusing on financial crime, fraud analytics, and regulatory compliance in European markets.

events.title: London Summit on Financial Crime & Sanctions 2025
events.description: High-level summit bringing together regulators, banks, and academics to discuss sanctions evasion and financial crime trends.

events.title: European Forensic Accounting & Fraud Detection Workshop
events.description: Academic workshop on forensic accounting techniques, financial statement fraud, and litigation support.

events.title: Global Fraud & Integrity Forum 2025
events.description: A global gathering of fraud experts, auditors, and academics covering corporate fraud, whistleblowing, and enforcement.

events.title: Latin America Tax Evasion & Informality Conference
events.description: Academic conference on tax evasion, informality, and enforcement in Latin American economies.

events.title: Asia-Pacific AML & RegTech Forum 2026
events.description: Regional forum on anti-money laundering technologies, RegTech solutions, and cross-border information sharing.

events.title: EU Workshop on Cryptocurrency Fraud & Market Abuse
events.description: Specialized workshop on crypto-asset fraud, market manipulation, and consumer protection in the EU.

events.title: African Financial Crime & Inclusion Forum
events.description: Conference exploring the balance between financial inclusion and financial crime controls in African markets.

events.title: Global Symposium on Corruption and Governance 2024
events.description: Annual symposium on corruption, governance, and institutional reform, with a track on financial sector corruption.

events.title: Oceania Forum on Financial Integrity & Fraud Risk
events.description: Regional event for researchers and practitioners focused on fraud risk and financial integrity in Oceania.

authors.affiliation: Centre for Financial Crime Studies, University of Madrid
authors.bio: Elena Martinez is a professor of economics specializing in anti-money laundering (AML) regulations, bank reporting incentives, and the effectiveness of suspicious activity reports in Europe.

authors.affiliation: School of Economics, Seoul National University
authors.bio: David Kim is an applied econometrician whose research focuses on cross-border money laundering, trade-based money laundering, and the use of big data for AML supervision in Asia-Pacific.
Sofia Rossi is a forensic accounting scholar examining financial statement fraud, earnings manipulation, and audit quality in European listed firms.

authors.affiliation: Institute for Public Finance, University of Sao Paulo
authors.bio: Luis Fernando Costa studies corporate tax evasion, profit shifting, and enforcement strategies in Latin American economies using administrative tax data.

authors.affiliation: Centro de Estudios Tributarios, Universidad de Chile
authors.bio: Maria Gonzalez is a public finance economist focusing on personal income tax evasion, VAT compliance gaps, and randomized audits in Latin America.
Peter Schneider researches bank fraud, risk-weight manipulation, and the use of supervisory data to detect financial statement irregularities in banks.

authors.affiliation: Graduate School of Economics, University of Tokyo
authors.bio: Akiko Tanaka studies cryptocurrency markets, exchange governance, and fraud, with work on wash trading, Ponzi-like token schemes, and market manipulation.

authors.affiliation: Department of Economics, University of Melbourne
authors.bio: James Owens is an empirical researcher on Ponzi schemes, investment fraud, and retail investor behavior, combining court records with transaction-level data.
Claire Dubois focuses on payment card economics, credit card fraud, and the design of incentives for merchants and issuers to invest in fraud prevention.

authors.affiliation: Federal University of Rio de Janeiro
authors.bio: Ricardo Silva specializes in money laundering through international trade, shell companies, and offshore centers, with a focus on Latin American trade corridors.

authors.affiliation: National Research University Higher School of Economics
authors.bio: Anna Kuznetsova studies corruption, informal payments, and bribery using household and firm surveys in transition economies.

authors.affiliation: Middle East Institute of Finance and Governance
authors.bio: Mohammed Al-Faruqi is a specialist in money laundering through informal value transfer systems, remittances, and cross-border cash smuggling.
Isabel Rodriguez works at the intersection of policy and research on AML directives, beneficial ownership transparency, and financial crime risk assessments in the EU.

authors.affiliation: Data Science Lab, University of Toronto
authors.bio: Thomas Nguyen develops machine learning models for fraud detection using large-scale transaction and network data from banks and online platforms.

authors.affiliation: Department of Economics, London School of Economics
authors.bio: Sara Patel is an economist of crime studying tax compliance, deterrence, and information interventions using randomized controlled trials and administrative tax data.
George Miller researches benchmark manipulation, LIBOR and other reference rates, and the impact of financial misconduct on market integrity.

publications.title: Decentralized Exchange Wash Trading and Cryptocurrency Fraud
publications.abstract: This working paper documents the prevalence of wash trading and associated fraudulent volume inflation on decentralized exchanges. Using transaction-level data from major Ethereum-based platforms, we develop detection algorithms that exploit self-trading patterns, linked-wallet clusters, and timing irregularities. We estimate that up to 47 percent of reported volume is artificially generated, and we quantify investor losses associated with fraudulent initial liquidity campaigns.

publications.title: Ponzi-Like Token Schemes in DeFi Lending Markets
publications.abstract: We study the emergence of Ponzi-like token schemes in decentralized lending platforms. By combining on-chain transaction graphs with protocol-level data, we identify self-referential collateral patterns and endogenous price support mechanisms characteristic of Ponzi dynamics. Our results show that these schemes disproportionately attract unsophisticated retail investors and are associated with extreme return volatility and rapid collapses.

publications.title: Cryptocurrency Fraud and Retail Investor Losses in Emerging Markets
publications.abstract: This paper quantifies retail investor losses from cryptocurrency fraud in emerging markets. Using a database of reported scam ICOs, rug pulls, and exchange exit scams linked to local bank and mobile money channels, we estimate aggregate losses and analyze their distribution across demographic groups. Difference-in-differences evidence suggests that targeted disclosure campaigns significantly reduce exposure to fraudulent schemes.

publications.title: Machine Learning Flags and Financial Statement Fraud: Evidence from European Firms
publications.abstract: We develop a machine learning model to flag financial statement fraud in a panel of European listed firms. Using restatements, enforcement actions, and forensic investigations to label fraud cases, we show that our model outperforms traditional accrual-based indicators, particularly for banks and complex conglomerates. We also examine how auditors respond to model-generated risk scores.

publications.title: Audit Partner Rotation and the Detection of Financial Statement Fraud
publications.abstract: This paper examines whether mandatory audit partner rotation improves the detection of financial statement fraud. Using a difference-in-differences design around rotation mandates, we find that rotation increases the probability that misstatements are detected and corrected, particularly for clients with weak internal controls. Machine learning models trained on partner-client match histories provide complementary evidence.

publications.title: Textual Analysis of Annual Reports for Financial Statement Fraud Detection
publications.abstract: We apply natural language processing methods to annual reports to detect financial statement fraud. Training on a labeled dataset of fraud and non-fraud firms, we show that textual features such as obfuscation, readability, and sentiment significantly improve fraud prediction accuracy relative to financial ratios alone. Our approach is particularly effective in banking and insurance sectors.

publications.title: Deep Learning Models for Financial Statement Fraud Detection in the Banking Sector
publications.abstract: Using a global panel of bank financial statements, we estimate deep learning models to detect financial statement fraud and earnings manipulation. Our models exploit non-linear relationships across hundreds of balance sheet and income statement items and substantially outperform benchmark logistic regressions. We also provide interpretability tools to identify key red flags for supervisors.

publications.title: Randomized Tax Audits and Evasion in Latin America
publications.abstract: We analyze randomized tax audit programs implemented in three Latin American countries. Using administrative tax return data matched to third-party reports, we estimate the impact of audit probability on reported income, deductions, and subsequent compliance. The results show substantial evasion in the absence of audits and highlight the importance of third-party information for effective enforcement.

publications.title: LIBOR Manipulation and the Breakdown of Reference Rate Integrity
publications.abstract: This article documented evidence of coordinated manipulation of LIBOR submissions by panel banks and assessed the impact on derivative pricing and market integrity. Following the discovery of serious data and disclosure irregularities, the article was formally retracted by the journal. The original empirical results should no longer be relied upon.

publications.title: Household Losses in Ponzi Schemes: Evidence from Court Records
publications.abstract: Using detailed court records from major Ponzi schemes, this working paper quantifies household-level losses and examines the demographic and financial characteristics of victims. We show that victims are more likely to be older, less financially literate, and connected through local social networks, highlighting the importance of trust in the spread of fraudulent schemes.

publications.title: Network Contagion in Ponzi Scheme Participation
publications.abstract: This paper studies the diffusion of Ponzi schemes through social networks. Using matched investor and referral data, we estimate peer effects in participation and show that early entrants with high centrality play a disproportionate role in the spread of fraudulent investment opportunities.

publications.title: Regulatory Responses to Ponzi Schemes After the Global Financial Crisis
publications.abstract: We document changes in regulatory responses to Ponzi schemes in the decade following the global financial crisis. Using a cross-country dataset of enforcement actions and new legislation, we show that jurisdictions with stronger investor protection and disclosure rules experience shorter scheme durations and smaller aggregate losses.

publications.title: Do Suspicious Activity Reports Deter Money Laundering? Evidence from Reporting Thresholds
publications.abstract: Exploiting regulatory changes in suspicious activity report (SAR) thresholds, we estimate the deterrent effect of enhanced reporting on money laundering activity. Using bank-level SAR filings and transaction data, we find that tighter reporting requirements reduce cash-intensive deposits and unusual cross-border transfers, with limited impact on legitimate activity.

publications.title: Bank Branch Closures and the Geography of Money Laundering Risk
publications.abstract: This working paper examines how bank branch closures affect the spatial distribution of money laundering risk. Using panel data on branch networks, transaction monitoring alerts, and SARs, we find that closures shift high-risk activity to neighboring jurisdictions and to non-bank financial institutions, challenging the effectiveness of location-based AML supervision.

publications.title: Trade-Based Money Laundering in Latin American Commodity Exports
publications.abstract: We construct novel indicators of trade-based money laundering (TBML) using customs data for Latin American commodity exporters. By comparing reported trade flows with partner-country statistics and market prices, we identify anomalous shipping patterns consistent with TBML. The results highlight specific product-country pairs and trade routes associated with elevated risk.

publications.title: Shell Companies and Offshore Ownership Chains in Money Laundering Cases
publications.abstract: Using leaked beneficial ownership registries linked to money laundering investigations, this article maps ownership chains that pass through offshore jurisdictions. We document common structures used to conceal proceeds of crime and assess the impact of beneficial ownership transparency reforms on the complexity of these chains.

publications.title: Informal Value Transfer Systems and AML Enforcement
publications.abstract: This working paper studies informal value transfer systems such as hawala and their interaction with formal anti-money laundering (AML) regimes. Combining qualitative fieldwork with supervisory data, we show how enforcement pressure in the formal banking system can shift money laundering activity to informal channels and discuss policy options for integrated oversight.

publications.title: Risk-Based AML Assessments in the European Union: Evidence from National Reports
publications.abstract: We compile and analyze national risk assessments of money laundering and terrorist financing across EU member states. Using a common coding framework, we compare identified risk factors, sectoral vulnerabilities, and planned mitigation measures. We highlight substantial cross-country heterogeneity in both methodology and depth of analysis and discuss implications for supranational AML coordination.
